title: Analog Quantum Asynchronous Event-Based Graph Neural Network
authors: Kristian Sotirov, Shaheen Acheche, Antonio A. Gentile, Osvaldo Simeone
abstract: Asynchronous, event-based graph neural networks (AEGNNs) have recently emerged as an efficient paradigm for processing the sparse and high-temporal-resolution data from event cameras. In this paper, we propose quantum analog AEGNNs (QA-AEGNNs), a novel framework to implement an AEGNN on a neutral-atom quantum computer. Neutral-atom quantum processors offer a programmable analog quantum computing platform based on controllable Rydberg-atom interactions. To this end, we map the streaming event data to an array of trapped neutral atoms, where each atom represents a graph node (event) and is positioned such that geometric proximity reflects the spatio-temporal neighborhood of events. The native Rydberg Hamiltonian of the quantum processor is programmed to mirror the message-passing computations of the AEGNN, with atomic qubit states serving as node feature embeddings and inter-atom interactions realizing graph edges. Furthermore, we propose a hybrid quantum-classical training scheme in which the analog Hamiltonian parameters (e.g., laser pulse amplitudes and detunings) are optimized using classical feedback to learn the quantum AEGNN model from data. Our approach leverages the continuous Hamiltonian dynamics and massive parallelism of neutral-atom quantum systems to natively execute event-based graph computations with potential accuracy improvements
summary: The paper introduces QA-AEGNN, a novel framework that implements asynchronous event-based graph neural networks on neutral-atom quantum computers. By programming the Rydberg Hamiltonian to mimic message passing via controlled atomic interactions, it achieves an analog quantum computation of GNNs. This approach demonstrates high accuracy and robustness compared to classical methods for processing neuromorphic vision data.
main_problem: The core problem is developing an efficient quantum analog for processing sparse, high-temporal-resolution event data using Graph Neural Networks (GNNs). This requires mapping the asynchronous graph structure onto a controllable physical quantum system.
main_result: The proposed Quantum Analog AEGNN (QA-AEGNN) successfully implements GNN message passing by programming the native Rydberg Hamiltonian dynamics of neutral atoms. It demonstrates superior performance compared to classical counterparts on both synthetic and neuromorphic datasets.
method: A hybrid quantum-classical training scheme is employed where analog Hamiltonian parameters are optimized classically. The graph computation is performed via time evolution governed by the Rydberg interaction, which naturally realizes message passing across physically placed atoms.
model_or_system: The system utilizes neutral-atom quantum processors with qubits encoded in ground/Rydberg states. The dynamics are governed by a programmable Rydberg Hamiltonian incorporating global drives, local detunings (for feature embedding), and strong pairwise van der Waals interactions ($V_{ij}$) that enforce graph connectivity via the Rydberg blockade.
key_observables: Test accuracy on classification tasks (Graph Classification, N-MNIST); final observable feature vector $\mathbf{z}_s$ constructed as a histogram of excited atoms after quantum evolution.
important_parameters: Rydberg blockade radius ($R_b$), Rabi frequency ($\Omega(t)$), local detuning parameters ($\delta_i(t)$), and the time evolution duration ($\Delta	au_s$).
main_assumptions: The physical placement of atoms must accurately map graph adjacency (neighbors within $R_b$) to ensure correct message passing. The framework assumes the ability to perform quantum state readout as a histogram.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the pipeline: event embedding $ightarrow$ Hamiltonian evolution ($\Omega(t), R_b$) $ightarrow$ Readout. Results compare QA-AEGNN vs AEGNN performance across different noise models and data types (e.g., Fig 6, Fig 7).
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the need for quantum GNNs on event data. It details the physical mapping onto neutral atoms using the Rydberg Hamiltonian to simulate message passing. The methodology covers feature encoding (state initialization vs. detuning) and the hybrid training loop. Finally, performance is benchmarked against classical methods under various noise conditions.
why_interesting: This work directly applies complex many-body physics—specifically controlled Rydberg interactions—to solve a cutting-edge problem in AI/ML (event-based GNNs). It showcases how continuous Hamiltonian dynamics can serve as a powerful, native analog computational primitive for information processing beyond simple quantum gates.
